coredata.dc:title: CDs@ZnO multi-core@shell structure: Rational designed versatile platform for multiple wavelength RTP, TADF and lasing in high security anti-counterfeiting

coredata.dc:description: The rational design of a versatile platform capable of producing multi-wavelength afterglow carbon dots (CDs) with varied structures remains a significant obstacle, particularly in enabling the concurrent realization of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). Here, a novel approach is proposed: the chemical encapsulation of CDs within a ZnO multi-core@shell structure to enable dual RTP and TADF emissions under visible light excitation. This is possible because a three-dimensional spatial constraint, coupled with chemical bonding for immobilization, introduces a pivotal role in stabilizing the triplet state within the CDs. Furthermore, CDs with diverse structures are validated to achieve multi-color afterglow emission through this versatile platform, which demonstrates broad applicability. Given the singlet excitons regeneration through the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process, random lasing is successfully realized from CDs@ZnO. Hence, the synergistic integration of RTP, TADF, and lasing construct the multi-levels anti-counterfeiting strategy, greatly enhance the security level. This work not only paves the way for the creation of afterglow materials and lasing media based on CDs but also holds potential for applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting techniques, encryption methods, and optical devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the formation of the multi-core@shell CDs@ZnO-25 structure.
a) HRTEM image of CDs@ZnO-25, inset shows the local magnified image. b) XRD patterns of CDs, ZnO and CDs@ZnO-25. c) FTIR spectra of CDs, ZnO and CDs@ZnO-25. d) XPS survey of CDs and CDs@ZnO-25.

a) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs@ZnO-25. b) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs@ZnO with different content of ZnO. c) Lifetime of phosphorescence emission of CDs@ZnO-25. d) photographs of CDs and CDs@ZnO taken before and after removal of the excitation source (405 nm). e) Delayed emission spectra of CDs@ZnO-25 collected at different temperatures. f) Afterglow photographs of CDs@ZnO-25 taken at different temperatures. g) schematic of energy-level structure of CDs. h) schematic of energy-level structure of CDs@ZnO. (Abs. = Absorption, FL = Fluorescence, Vib. = Vibration, Phos. = Phosphorescence).

Afterglow photographs of a) E-CDs@ZnO-25 and b) D-CDs@ZnO-25 taken at different temperatures. c) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of E-CDs@ZnO-25. The insets are the photographs of E-CDs@ZnO-25 under daylight (top) and 405 nm laser excitation (bottom). d) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of D-CDs@ZnO-25. The insets are the photographs of D-CDs@ZnO-25 under daylight (top) and 405 nm laser excitation (bottom). Delayed emission spectra of e) E-CDs@ZnO-25 and f) D-CDs@ZnO-25 collected at different temperatures. Lifetimes of phosphorescence emission of g) E-CDs@ZnO-25 and h) D-CDs@ZnO-25.

a) Schematic of lasing detection. b) Schematic of light scattering in CDs@ZnO-25. Lasing spectra of c) CDs@ZnO-25, d) CDs@ZnO-5 and PL spectra of e) CDs@SiO2. Output-input curves of f) CDs@ZnO-25, g) CDs@ZnO-5 and h) CDs@SiO2.

a) Number of each pattern and photographs of the patterns under daylight and 405 nm laser. b) Schematic of the anti-counterfeiting process. c) Schematic of lasing coding anti-counterfeiting. d) Photographs of the pattern under the thermochromic afterglow process. e) Temperature-dependent anti-counterfeiting pattern. f) Demonstration of the encryption made from CDs@ZnO-25, CDs@ZnO-5 and CDs@SiO2. (‘Pol’, ‘y’, ‘u’ are made of CDs@ZnO-25; ‘e’, ‘o’ are made of CDs@ZnO-5; ‘it’, ‘th’ are made of CDs@SiO2).

head.ce:title: CDs@ZnO multi-core@shell structure: Rational designed versatile platform for multiple wavelength RTP, TADF and lasing in high security anti-counterfeiting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational design of a versatile platform capable of producing multi-wavelength afterglow carbon dots (CDs) with varied structures remains a significant obstacle, particularly in enabling the concurrent realization of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). Here, a novel approach is proposed: the chemical encapsulation of CDs within a ZnO multi-core@shell structure to enable dual RTP and TADF emissions under visible light excitation. This is possible because a three-dimensional spatial constraint, coupled with chemical bonding for immobilization, introduces a pivotal role in stabilizing the triplet state within the CDs. Furthermore, CDs with diverse structures are validated to achieve multi-color afterglow emission through this versatile platform, which demonstrates broad applicability. Given the singlet excitons regeneration through the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process, random lasing is successfully realized from CDs@ZnO. Hence, the synergistic integration of RTP, TADF, and lasing construct the multi-levels anti-counterfeiting strategy, greatly enhance the security level. This work not only paves the way for the creation of afterglow materials and lasing media based on CDs but also holds potential for applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting techniques, encryption methods, and optical devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow emission is a phenomenon where light is emitted from optical materials after the removal of the excitation source, such as thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) [1] and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). Due to its advantageous delayed luminescence characteristics and high signal-to-noise ratio in the time dimension [2], afterglow emission finds applications in optical devices [3], anti-counterfeiting and information encryption [4], displays [5], and sensors [6]. Specifically, organic TADF emitters can harness excitons from both the singlet and triplet states, enabling the production of Organic Light-Emitting Diodes (OLEDs) with internal quantum efficiencies (IQE) of up to 100 %.[7] TADF is therefore regarded as a highly promising mechanism for efficiently harvesting excitons in OLED applications [8].

In traditional inorganic afterglow materials, noble metal ions and rare earth elements are employed to achieve afterglow emission, leveraging their unique electronic structures that facilitate spin–orbit coupling [9]. However, the high cost, scarcity, and potential environmental toxicity of these materials pose significant barriers to their widespread use in various applications. Carbon dots (CDs)-based afterglow materials have recently garnered significant attention due to their eco-friendly preparation, cost-effectiveness, and exceptional optical performance [10]. Various strategies have been proposed to achieve efficient afterglow emission from CDs, including crosslinking for enhanced emission [11,12], the construction of rigid hydrogen bond network structures [13], and embedding within rigid matrices [14,15]. However, the predominant CDs-based afterglow materials typically involve the encapsulation of CDs within matrices [16]. This strategy serves to mitigate environmental quenching agents such as water and oxygen, while the simple structure in which the CDs are solely surrounded by a whole matrix may only provide limited stabilization to the singlet and triplet state. Consequently, these materials generally exhibit either RTP or TADF, but not both simultaneously. In addition, the competitive nature between these two mechanisms presents a notable challenge in achieving both RTP and TADF concurrently within a single-material system [17]. This challenge has led to a limited number of reports on CDs-based materials that exhibit both RTP and TADF properties simultaneously [18]. The concurrent presence of RTP and TADF compensates for the insufficient luminescent intensity of RTP at higher temperatures and addresses the weakness of TADF at lower temperatures, thereby achieving efficient afterglow emission across a range of temperatures. Furthermore, this type of materials exhibits temperature-dependent luminescent properties, enhancing its versatility in practical applications and broadening its scope of utility.

Recently, Lu et al. reported four types of CDs with similar structures to achieve full-color afterglow emission. Surprisingly, one of these exhibited dual-mode emission with both RTP and TADF [18]. This study introduces an innovative synthesis protocol for dual-mode afterglow emission materials. However, exploration of a universal strategy for CDs with different structures is still necessary. Lin et al. embedded the CDs into the cyanuric acid (CA) matrix, which is applicable to many kinds of CDs for achieving or enhancing their afterglow performances. In particular, the o-CDs, synthesized from o-phenylenediamine, exhibit infrared RTP and green TADF simultaneously [19]. Nevertheless, this strategy is unable to assure sufficiently high efficiency of the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process, leading to the incomplete transversion from RTP to TADF when increasing the temperatures, which limit the further application of the CDs-based materials. CDs typically derive from a variety of precursors, can be doped with diverse elements, and are synthesized through distinct methods, leading to complex and diverse structures [20]. The varying architectures of CDs can endow them with a wide range of photoluminescent properties. Developing a universal approach that is applicable to induce the afterglow emission from CDs with different structures is crucial for the fabrication of afterglow materials exhibiting various luminescent characteristics. Continued investigation into an efficient platform for CDs with different structures that can facilitate both the intersystem crossing (ISC) and RISC processes with high efficiency remains essential. Furthermore, most previous afterglow materials are designed to function under ultraviolet excitation [21]. In contrast to ultraviolet light sources, visible light sources are more common and pose a lower risk to human health, rendering visible light-excitable CDs-based afterglow materials more suitable for practical use. However, research on visible light-excitable CDs-based afterglow materials remains limited at present [22].

In this study, we introduce an innovative and effective strategy where the integration of CDs within the ZnO matrix enables the simultaneous realization of dual-mode afterglow emission, encompassing RTP and TADF. CDs are simply synthesized using citric acid, without intentional doping. Following a straightforward thermal treatment, the CDs are successfully encapsulated within the ZnO multi-core@shell structure. Surprisingly, the CDs@ZnO exhibit thermochromic afterglow properties, transitioning from yellow to green as temperatures increase, under visible light excitation. This afterglow emission is attributed to the stabilization of energy levels, a result of the collaborative effect of the ZnO multi-core@shell structure and C-O-Zn bonds. The formation of C-O-Zn bonds occurs between the –COOH groups from CDs and the –OH groups from ZnO. The CDs@ZnO also demonstrate a 25-fold increase in PL intensity compared to the CDs solution. Furthermore, the ZnO multi-core@shell structure is validated as a rational designed versatile platform for CDs with diverse structure, facilitating the achievement of the multi-wavelength afterglow emission. Through this platform, different types of CDs are capable of exhibiting RTP, and even the concurrent attainment of TADF becomes feasible for the CDs with appropriate energy level structure. Additionally, due to the regeneration of singlet excitons through the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process, random lasing of CDs@ZnO is achieved.

To enhance security levels further, we introduce a novel CDs-based multi-level anti-counterfeiting strategy for the first time, combining thermochromic afterglow properties with random lasing spectra. Different color combinations can be displayed by adjusting temperatures, and the lasing coding can be modified by varying the pump power. This study not only presents a rational design for afterglow materials but also offers insights for enhancing security levels in anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

The CDs are synthesized through a simple pyrolysis method by using citric acid (CA) as precursor. When mixed with CDs solution, the ZnO particles undergo facile hydrolysis within the acidic environment, and the subsequent thermal treatment is able to enhance this process. It has been observed that hydrolysis initiates at the periphery of ZnO particles, resulting in their conversion to Zn (OH)2. Subsequent to the complete evaporation of the aqueous medium at elevated temperatures, the dehydration of Zn (OH)2 takes place, culminating in the reformation of a rigid ZnO shell. Concurrently, ZnO particles that undergo incomplete hydrolysis serve as the core, which are subsequently encapsulated within the ZnO shell in conjunction with the CDs. Ultimately, following thermal processing, the ZnO microparticles coalesce to form a macroscale multi-core@shell structure. It is highlighted that, following the process of hydrolysis, abundant –OH groups are generated on both the surface of ZnO particles and the ZnO shell, which can react with the COOH groups on the CDs to form the robust C(O)OZn bonds. The CDs are thus intercalated between the ZnO particles and the ZnO shell, secured through the rigid C(O)OZn bonds, as shown in Fig. 1 . The rigid multi-core@shell structure effectively protects the CDs and stabilizes the energy levels of CDs. As a consequence, significant PL enhancement and the concurrence of the RTP and TADF are astonishingly observed from the CDs@ZnO-25. This revelation underscores the critical role of the ZnO matrix in improving the optical behaviors of CDs, opening new avenues for advanced optical materials.

In order to verify the structure transformation of ZnO, SEM images of ZnO and CDs@ZnO-25 are investigated. As exhibited in Fig. S2a, the sizes of ZnO particles are less than 10 μm, while the sizes of CDs@ZnO-25 become larger than 100 μm after the thermal treatment (Fig. S2b and c). The morphological structure of CDs is characterized by TEM images shown in Fig. S2d. Abundant monodispersed nanoparticles can be observed with average sizes of 2.5 nm. From the HRTEM image shown in Fig. S2e, the CDs exhibit crystallinity with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facets of graphite.[23] For TEM test, the CDs@ZnO-25 with macrostructure are ground into smaller size. Fig. 2a and S2f demonstrate the microscopic structure of CDs@ZnO-25, in which the core–shell boundary of ZnO can be clearly witnessed. Subsequent to encapsulation by the ZnO matrix, the CDs exhibit a tendency to aggregate due to their substantial quantity, rendering them indistinguishable in TEM images. Nevertheless, the presence of CDs within the ZnO matrix can be ascertained based on their distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is at variance with the lattice spacing of ZnO (0.26 nm) [24], as evidenced in Fig. 2 a. To substantiate the presence of the CDs, Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) analyses are conducted, as depicted in Fig. S3a. The CDs@ZnO-25 are found to comprise C, O, and Zn elements, with the C content attributed to the CDs. The elemental mapping reveals a uniform distribution of C across the sample, in concert with the Zn and O elements (Fig. S3b-f). This distribution pattern suggests that the CDs are densely arranged within the ZnO multi-core@shell structure following encapsulation.

Fig. 2b depicts the XRD patterns of CDs, ZnO and CDs@ZnO-25. The broad diffraction peak of CDs around 20° is assigned to the (002) planes of carbon [25]. However, no obvious sharp diffraction peaks are found from CDs, which may be attributed to the severe structural damages during the oxidation process [26]. The diffraction pattern observed from ZnO is in alignment with the reference data (JCPDS# 70–2551), confirming the crystalline planes of hexagonal ZnO wurtzite structure including (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201) and (202). It is evident that the pattern of CDs@ZnO-25 displays all the peaks exhibited in spectra of CDs and ZnO, which illustrates that the combination makes no obvious influence on the crystallinity and phase purity of these two materials. Analysis of the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) manifests the chemical structure of CDs, ZnO and CDs@ZnO-25 (Fig. 2c). For CDs, the peaks at 1400, 1690, 2925 and 3430 cm−1 can be originated from C-O, –COOH, C-H and –OH, respectively [27]. The sharp peak of ZnO at 540 cm−1 is attributed to Zn-O. And the peak of –OH centering at 3430 cm−1 can be also observed in the spectrum of ZnO. Notably, for CDs@ZnO-25, the peaks of COOH and –OH disappear and the peaks of C=O and C-O-Zn, located at 1544 cm−1 and 650 cm−1 respectively, arise [28]. This spectral evidence indicates the reaction between –COOH from CDs and the –OH from ZnO, which ultimately forms the C(O)-O-Zn linkage. In addition, as the abundant defects present after the combination of CDs and ZnO, which weaken the strength of Zn-O bond [29], the peak of Zn-O lies in the lower wavenumbers of 453 cm−1.

Fig. 2d demonstrates the XPS survey of CDs and CDs@ZnO-25. The main elements of CDs consist of C and O, with the addition of Zn occurring after combination with ZnO. The C1s high resolution spectrum of CDs reveals four peaks at 289.0 eV, 286.1 eV, 285.0 eV and 284.4 eV, corresponding to COOH, C-O, C-C and C=C, respectively (Fig. S4a) [29,30]. The O1s high resolution spectrum of CDs shown in Fig. S4b exhibits three main peaks at 533.4 eV, 532.7 eV and 532 eV, which can be ascribed to H-O-H, C-O and C=O, respectively [31]. For CDs@ZnO-25, two peaks centered at 1045 eV and 1022 eV, exhibited in the Zn2p high resolution spectrum, are assigned to Zn2p 1/2 and Zn2p 3/2 respectively, which suggests the Zn2+ species are in a completely oxidized state (Fig. S4c) [32]. The C1s high resolution spectrum of CDs@ZnO-25 reveals four distinct peaks of O-C=O, C-O, C-C and C=C (Fig. S4d). It is pertinent to mention that the peak related to COOH group undergoes a shift to 288.6 eV from initial 289 eV, which is ascribed to the O-C=O group signifying the conversion of COOH to C(O)OZn [33]. As for the O1s orbit, the peak of H-O-H disappears since the water is evaporated during the thermal treatment, while the peak of Zn-O at 531.6 eV arises (Fig. S4e) [32]. Based on the results discussed above, we hypothesize that the CDs are synthesized with a great amount of –COOH groups by using CA as raw material. Following the thermal treatment, the aqueous medium is completely evaporated, facilitating the embedding of the CDs within the ZnO matrix via the processes of hydrolysis and dehydration of ZnO. The reaction between –COOH (CDs) and –OH (ZnO) occurs and thus forms the C-O-Zn linkage. The ZnO particles finally coalesce into a macroscale rigid framework, affording the protection to CDs with the establishment of C-O-Zn bonds.

To study the optical properties of CDs and CDs@ZnO-25, PL spectra are recorded at various excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S5a, the excitation dependence can be observed obviously, which is a general encountered feature of CDs.[34] As the excitation wavelengths increase, a peak at 395 nm can be found in excitation spectrum, corresponding yielding the most intense emission at 500 nm (Fig. S5b). The CDs aqueous solution is pale yellow under daylight and performs cyan luminescence under 405 nm irradiation (the inset of Fig. S5b). After combination with the ZnO multi-core@shell structure, the CDs@ZnO-25 also show the excitation dependence, similar excitation peak and optimal emission at 420 nm and 520 nm, respectively, which indicates that the PL emission is derived from CDs in CDs@ZnO-25 (Fig. S5c and d). Furthermore, PL spectra of ZnO with and without thermal treatment at 150 ℃ are also compared (Fig. S6a). No emission from ZnO can be seen by excited at 430 nm, suggesting that the PL of CDs@ZnO-25 is originated from CDs but not ZnO. Additionally, we use the melted citric acid, which is treated at 250 ℃ for 2 min, to modify the ZnO. However, only weak blue light can be witnessed from the sample (the inset of Fig. S6a). This is because only a few fluorescent molecules are generated during the short-term thermal treatment without formation of CDs, which rules out the possibility of luminescence caused by defect of ZnO induced by dopants. The results shown above obviously explain that the luminescence of CDs@ZnO-25 is originated from CDs, which also reveal the existence of CDs within the ZnO multi-core@shell structure. It is noted that, the CDs@ZnO-25 show light yellow under daylight and cyan luminescence under 405 nm irradiation, while the cyan light results from 405 nm excitation laser and 520 nm emission of CDs@ZnO-25 (the inset of Fig. S5d).

To explore the optimal condition of preparation, PL spectra of CDs@ZnO with varying ZnO content and treatment temperatures are examined. As shown in Fig. S6b, it is observed that the CDs@ZnO-25 exhibits a superior PL intensity compared to CDs@ZnO-5. This implies that an increased concentration of ZnO within the material leads to a more substantial enhancement of its optical performance. In addition, the PL intensity decreases with increasing the treatment temperature, which is due to the further oxidation and reduced fluorophores of CDs [35]. Especially for 350 ℃, most of the chromophore moieties on CDs are thermally removed at this temperature, resulting in a dramatical decline in PL intensity (Fig. S6c). Hence, mass ratio of 1:0.25 (CDs solution: ZnO) and 150 ℃ is chosen as the final ZnO content and treatment temperature, respectively, for further investigation of CDs@ZnO-25. Fig. S7a delineates the PL spectra of CDs at different temperatures, which have been pre-dried naturally. The PL intensity exhibits a marked decline with increasing temperature. Concurrently, a pronounced redshift in the emission peak is observed, which is attributable to the narrowing of the bandgap in the CDs resulting from oxidation at elevated temperatures, as depicted in Fig. S7b [36]. In comparison to the CDs, both the CDs@ZnO-5 and CDs@ZnO-25 demonstrate a more gradual reduction in intensity and superior thermal stability, as evidenced in Fig. S7c and e. Notably, the CDs@ZnO-25 retains 65 % of its initial intensity at 250 °C, whereas the retention is only 0.6 % for CDs and 10 % for CDs@ZnO-5 (Fig. S7f). It is intriguing to note that the emission wavelengths of the CDs@ZnO-25 remain largely invariant with the escalation of temperature, as depicted in Fig. S7d. This is attributed to the robust multi-core@shell architecture of CDs@ZnO-25, which effectively resists external oxidation and stabilizes the energy levels, thereby reducing non-radiative transitions and enhancing thermal stability.

It can be seen that the CDs show an absorption peak at 253 nm with a shoulder at 327 nm from Fig. S8a. The peak of 253 nm is attributed to π-π* transitions of C=C, while the peak of 327 nm is related to n-π* transitions of C=O bonds [37]. However, the absorption peak of CDs exhibits a blue shift to 240 nm, with a broader absorption band in the region of 320 nm to 800 nm. It is noted that the change of peak at 327 nm may be due to the transformation of –COOH to C(O)-O-Zn (Fig. 1), which leads to a stronger absorption in visible region. The improvement of absorption plays an important role in PL enhancement, since the best excitation wavelength is located in visible region. This result indicates that the energy levels of CDs are stabilized, thereby facilitating improved absorption and emission. It is well known that aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect is always a serious problem for CDs. Dispersion of CDs in solid matrices is generally used to mitigate the ACQ effect for enhancement of PL intensity.[38] To verify that the weakening of ACQ effect is not the main factor of PL enhancement from CDs@ZnO-25, PL spectra of CDs dispersed on the surface of other particles are also compared in Fig. S8b. As shown in Fig. S8b, it is noted that the PL intensity of CDs@GeO2 and CDs@SiO2 exhibits an enhancement of merely 2.8 and 4.1 times, respectively, relative to that of the CDs solution. In stark contrast, a remarkable enhancement of the PL intensity, amounting to 25-fold, is achieved for the CDs@ZnO-25. It can be observed that the PL intensity of CDs@GeO2 and CDs@SiO2 only improve to 2.8 and 4.1 times respectively, however, the enhancement of PL intensity as high as 25-fold can be obtained from CDs@ZnO-25. Fig. S8c exhibits the photographs of CDs, CDs@GeO2, CDs@SiO2 and CDs@ZnO-25 irradiated by 405 nm laser. The results above indicate that dispersion of CDs can only slightly increase the PL emission and is not the main factor of enhancement from CDs@ZnO-25. It is noted that, even the sample constructed by low quantity of ZnO can have better optical performance than that compounded with SiO2, which illustrates the validity of ZnO multi-core@shell structure (Fig. S6b).

As shown in Fig. S8d, both CDs and CDs@ZnO-25 exhibit biexponential decay with two lifetimes. CDs have lifetimes of 1.15 ns and 3.98 ns, while the CDs@ZnO-25 have significantly longer lifetimes of 2.14 ns and 6.77 ns. It can be ascribed to the stabilization effect of the rigid multi-core@shell structure of CDs@ZnO-25 to reduce the non-radiative transition. In addition, the PLQY of CDs is measured to be 0.53 % (Fig. S8e), while the PLQY of CDs@ZnO-25 demonstrates a 30-fold increase compared to that of CDs (Fig. S8f).

In order to study the RTP properties of CDs@ZnO, we measure and analyze the delayed emission spectra. Fig. 3 a shows the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs@ZnO-25, with peaks centered at 510 nm and 564 nm. Moreover, the phosphorescence emission can be observed from CDs@ZnO-5, which performs a RTP peak at a longer wavelength of 585 nm (Fig. S9a). Fig. 3b illustrates that the CDs@ZnO-5 demonstrates a notably diminished phosphorescent intensity in contrast to the CDs@ZnO-25. This observation aligns with the previously noted trend in fluorescent characteristics as depicted in Fig. S6b. The CDs@ZnO-25 exhibits a phosphorescence lifetime of 83 ms, as documented in Fig. 3c. In contrast, the CDs@ZnO-5 manifests a slightly reduced phosphorescence lifetime, which is recorded at 75 ms, as illustrated in Fig. S9b. The RTP photographs of CDs@ZnO-25 and CDs@ZnO-5 are displayed in Fig. 3d, which are visible to naked eyes for a duration of 1.1 s. Blue fluorescence is observed from CDs@ZnO-25 when excited by 405 nm laser, while the gradually decayed yellow phosphorescence can be seen after the termination of light source. And the CDs@ZnO-5 manifest a relatively diminished orange phosphorescence emission that persist for a shorter duration, as perceived by naked eyes.

Remarkably, the CDs@ZnO-25 demonstrate exceptional TADF properties and color modulation upon different temperatures. As exhibited in Fig. 3e, the delayed emission intensity decreases with increasing the temperature from 30 to 40 ℃, which is a typical feature of RTP [39]. Nevertheless, as the temperature is raised up to 60 ℃, there is an observed enhancement in intensity accompanied by a spectral shift of the emission peak from 564 nm to 510 nm, a hallmark of TADF [40]. It suggests that the delayed fluorescence becomes predominant. These results imply the concurrence of the RTP and TADF emission within the CDs@ZnO-25, which can be switched by varying the temperature. When the temperature is further elevated, non-radiative transition assumes a dominant role and thus leads to a diminution in the emission intensity. It can be observed that the CDs@ZnO-25 display luminous yellow afterglow with a persistence of approximately 1.3 s. With increasing the temperature, the color of afterglow undergoes a change from yellow to green yellow and then dark green. Concurrently, the duration of the afterglow pattern exhibits a progressive decrease (Fig. 3f). Interestingly, RTP and TADF emission can also be discovered from CDs@ZnO-5, as shown in Fig. S9c. However, no discernible alteration in color can be observed through increasing the temperature (Fig. S10). This is because the TADF emission is relatively faint in CDs@ZnO-5, while the non-radiative transition is prevalent, culminating in the TADF process being rendered inconspicuous. Concurrently, the energy bandgap of CDs@ZnO-5 becomes narrower when the temperature increases, leading to the redshift of emission peaks of both the TADF and RTP (Fig. S9c). It can be assigned to the inadequate presence of ZnO, which is insufficient to completely stabilize the energy levels of CDs, resembling the behavior observed in pristine CDs (Fig. S7b). Consequently, the color coordinates of thermochromic afterglow from CDs@ZnO-5 only exhibit a slight displacement, whereas those of the CDs@ZnO-25 undergo a pronounced transition from yellow to green area, as depicted on the CIE 1931 diagram (Fig. S11). As the important characteristics, lifetimes of CDs@ZnO-25 at 560 and 510 nm are investigated at different treatment temperatures (Fig. S12). With the increase of temperature, the lifetimes of both the phosphorescence and TADF exhibit continued decline due to the enhanced non-radiative transitions (Fig. S13). And the more rapid decrease of phosphorescence lifetime prior to the temperature reaching 50 ℃ can be attributed to the more pronounced attenuation of the phosphorescence emission from this range of temperature, which is consistent with the results of Fig. 3e.

In an effort to enhance the comprehension of the afterglow characteristics from CDs@ZnO-25, we also investigate the temperature-dependent afterglow spectra within the vacuum environment. Fig. S14 demonstrates that the CDs@ZnO-25 exhibit a distinct phosphorescent emission peak at 560 nm when the temperature is maintained at −150 ℃. As the temperature is elevated from −30 ℃ to 60 ℃, a progressive decline in the phosphorescent intensity at 560 nm is observed, accompanied by a broadening of the spectral bandwidth. This phenomenon suggests the coexistence of RTP and TADF. It is noteworthy that TADF becomes the predominant emission mechanism when the temperature surpasses 60 ℃, resulting in a blue shift of the afterglow emission peak and an increase in intensity [2,41]. This result is similar to that procured under ambient environmental settings (Fig. 3e).

Nonetheless, the critical transition temperature at which the afterglow emission peak completely shifts to 510 nm is elevated to 120 ℃ under vacuum conditions, in contrast to the transition observed at ambient conditions (60 ℃). This discrepancy can be attributed to the fact that the RTP emission undergoes quenching via a dynamic collisional process in the presence of molecular oxygen [42], which can be corroborated by the prolonged afterglow lifetime observed at high temperatures when the CDs@ZnO-25 are subjected to vacuum treatment (Fig. S13). Concurrently, the TADF and RTP processes are in competition. As a result, within the temperature range of 60 ℃ to 120 ℃, under oxygen-excluded conditions, RTP remains robust while TADF is suppressed. It is only at the threshold of 120 ℃ that TADF becomes dominant due to the accelerated decay of RTP induced by thermal perturbations and molecular motion.

Indeed, the case under ambient conditions is more conducive to practical applications, owing to the lower transition temperature and the less stringent environmental requirements. Consequently, the subsequent experiments were carried out under ambient environmental conditions.

Fig. S13 and S15 illustrate that the lifetime of the afterglow emission at 560 nm exhibits a consistent decline as the temperature is elevated. In contrast, at 510 nm, the afterglow lifetime initially rises with increasing temperature from −60 to 0°C, followed by a decrease as the temperature continues to ascend to 120°C. This behavior is indicative of the TADF.2.

As previously discussed, CDs containing numerous –COOH groups are incorporated into the ZnO matrix through the hydrolysis and dehydration of ZnO particles. The –OH groups on the surface of the ZnO matrix, formed during the hydrolysis process, interact with the –COOH groups of CDs to create strong C-O-Zn bonds. This process results in the formation of a macroscale, rigid ZnO multi-core@shell structure, effectively safeguarding the CDs through the linkage of C-O-Zn bonds.

The multi-core@shell structure effectively prevents the CDs from oxidation and simultaneously stabilizes the energy levels of the CDs, resulting in the appearance of RTP and TADF. Upon integration with CDs, the CDs@SiO2 and CDs@GeO2 composites maintain their original dimensions (Fig. S16). It suggests that no constitution of macroscale, rigid structure occurs from CDs@SiO2 and CDs@GeO2. The CDs are unable to manifest afterglow emission due to the inadequate stabilization of energy levels afforded by SiO2 and GeO2. This can be corroborated by the less significant reinforcement in PL intensity relative to that observed in the CDs@ZnO-25. Moreover, the enhanced thermal stability exhibited by CDs@ZnO-25 is also served as compelling evidence in support of this assertion (Fig. S7b, d and f).

To confirm the role of C-O-Zn bonds, we fabricate the Si-CDs referring to our previous work,[43] in which the –COOH groups of CDs are mostly substituted by organosilane functional groups. As shown in Fig. S17a, the Si-CDs perform the familiar excitation dependence characteristic. Two peaks of absorbance at 273 nm and 361 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of C=C and n-π* transitions of C=O, respectively (Fig. S17b). The excitation spectrum indicates two peaks at 465 nm and 504 nm, and the optimal emission peak at 560 nm can be found in the PL spectrum. It is worth noting that the C=O groups at 1660 cm−1 shown in the FTIR spectrum of both the Si-CDs and Si-CDs@ZnO-25 are derived from the acylamino, which is different from that of –COOH at 1720 cm−1 (Fig. S17c).[43] Moreover, the peak of C-O-Zn at 650 cm−1 is invisible in the FTIR spectrum of Si-CDs@ZnO-25, indicating that the replacement of organosilane functional groups greatly decrease the amount of –COOH and therefore successfully hinder the formation of C-O-Zn bonds. As expected, no afterglow emission can be witnessed from Si-CDs@ZnO-25. This is because the mere presence of ZnO multi-core@shell structure, lacking the establishment of C-O-Zn bonds to immobilize the CDs, is insufficient to stabilize the energy levels of triplet state for CDs.

Hence, it is expected that the rigid ZnO multi-core@shell structure cooperated with the robust C-O-Zn bonds effectively restricts the molecular motion of CDs@ZnO-25, and thus suppresses the non-radiative transition, stabilizing the energy levels of triplet state for CDs. Fig. 3g and h exhibit the schematic diagram of electronic transition from singlet state and triplet state. Initially, the electrons jump to the excited state (S1) from the ground state (S0). The excitons then undergo a facile transfer to triplet state (T1) by the inherent intersystem crossing (ISC) process. However, due to the spin-forbidden nature of the transition between singlet state (S1) and triplet state (T1), in conjunction with the high susceptibility of electrons to environmental factors such as water and oxygen, triplet state excitons are dissipated through vibration and interaction with dissolved oxygen in aqueous media (Fig. 3g). By means of integrating into the rigid multi-core@shell framework of ZnO matrix, the CDs are imposed confinement. The formation of the robust C-O-Zn bonds within the ZnO framework serves to further stabilize the triplet excited state, thereby enabling the occurrence of phosphorescence through spin–orbit coupling. The confinement effectively reduces the likelihood of non-radiative transitions by limiting molecular vibrations, which is crucial for prolonging the persistence of triplet state and the subsequent afterglow emission.[13,44,45]

At room temperature, the value of energy band gap between S1 and T1 (ΔEst) is calculated to be 0.23 eV by using equation (1). The narrow energy band gap (generally < 0.3 eV) facilitates the breaking of the spin-forbidden barrier, thereby enabling an efficient reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process.[46] The intense TADF emission becomes possible through the conversion of triplet excitons to singlet excitons, induced by thermal activation energy (Fig. 3h).[47] It is pertinent to mention that, no afterglow emission can be observed from the CDs, CDs@SiO2, CDs@GeO2 and Si-CDs@ZnO-25, while the CDs@ZnO-5 only exhibit a faint phosphorescence emission, accompanied with the unapparent TADF emission. All the results underscore the critical role of the rigid multi-core@shell structure of ZnO and the robust C-O-Zn bonds in facilitating both the ISC and RISC processes.

It is noted that, the ZnO multi-core@shell architecture is a rationally designed versatile platform for multi-wavelength afterglow emission from CDs, which can be fabricated by the carbon precursor with completely different chemical structure. Herein, two commonly used carbon source are selected: Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) and D-Glutamic acid, both rich in –COOH groups, are used as precursor materials for the synthesis of E-CDs and D-CDs, respectively.[48–50] As exhibited in Fig. 4 a and b, E-CDs@ZnO-25 and D-CDs@ZnO-25 display sandy brown and pale green afterglow, respectively, under different operation temperatures. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence peak of E-CDs@ZnO-25 at 555 nm, with a phosphorescence peak at 610 nm. Fluorescence peak of D-CDs@ZnO-25 is found at 470 nm, while the phosphorescence peak is measured at 505 nm (Fig. 4d). The insets within Fig. 4c and d present the photographs of E-CDs@ZnO-25 and D-CDs@ZnO-25, with white and blue light excited by 405 nm laser, respectively. Nevertheless, neither E-CDs@ZnO-25 nor D-CDs@ZnO-25 exhibits TADF emission as the temperature increases (Fig. 4e and f). This can be attributed to the existence of various functional groups, especially for the amino functional groups, which may facilitate the non-radiative energy transfer within the molecules. Hence, the RISC process is hindered, in spite of the ΔEst of E-CDs@ZnO-25 and D-CDs@ZnO-25 are measured below 0.3 eV (0.20 eV for E-CDs@ZnO-25 and 0.18 eV for D-CDs@ZnO-25). Conversely, the CDs from CDs@ZnO-25 are composed solely of the C and O elements, conduce to the sustenance of an efficient RISC pathway, and thus display RTP and TADF concurrently.

To verify the effect of nitrogen elements on afterglow emission, comparative experiments are conducted. We fabricate the NCDs-1@ZnO-25 and NCDs-2@ZnO-25 by using citric acid and urea as precursor materials. It is noted that the urea, serving as the nitrogen source, is introduced to adjust the nitrogen doping level, as reported before [51]. As a result, the NCDs-1@ZnO-25 exhibit a fluorescence peak at 520 nm, with a phosphorescence peak at 600 nm (Fig. S18a). Consistent with expectations, both the RTP and TADF emission are shown after doping of nitrogen elements (Fig. S18b). However, the TADF property of CDs becomes less obvious than that without nitrogen doping. With the elevation of temperatures, the afterglow color of NCDs-1@ZnO-25 undergoes transition from orange to yellow (Fig. S18d). After further increase of the nitrogen content, the TADF trait disappears from NCDs-2@ZnO-25 (Fig. S19b), which perform the similar PL and phosphorescence peaks as NCDs-1@ZnO-25 as shown in Fig. S19a. And no alteration of afterglow color is discernible with the increment in operating temperatures (Fig. S19d). Besides, the nitrogen doping evidently reduces the lifetimes of afterglow emission, concomitant with a decreased intensity of both the PL and RTP emission (Fig. S18c, S19c and S20a and b). The obtained results indicate the fact that nitrogen doping is detrimental to the effective RISC process, leading to the disappearance of TADF emission. The occurrence of non-radiative energy transfer mediated by amino functional groups within the molecular structure results in the dissipation of energy from the singlet state. Consequently, the performance of RTP and TADF is compromised.

In addition, the phosphorescence lifetimes of E-CDs@ZnO-25 and D-CDs@ZnO-25 are characterized by a bi-exponential decay model. The lifetimes for the E-CDs@ZnO-25 are determined to be τ1 = 18 ms and τ2 = 190 ms, whereas for the D-CDs@ZnO-25, the measured lifetimes are τ1 = 15 ms and τ2 = 177 ms. To sum up, we verify that the strategy for inducing the afterglow emission from CDs, in which the CDs are encapsulated within the ZnO multi-core@shell structure, is general for other CDs synthesized by various precursor materials. Additionally, the realization of afterglow materials exhibiting different luminous colors is feasible through the encapsulation of CDs possessing distinct emission wavelengths within the ZnO multi-core@shell structure, which provides guidance to rational design of multicolor afterglow materials.

Typically, the dissipation of excitons from the triplet state impedes population inversion in the singlet state, leading to significant optical losses (Fig. 3g). Consequently, the feasibility of lasing is compromised due to insufficient optical gain. To enhance the number of singlet excitons for light amplification, TADF is progressively integrated into lasing materials.[52,53] The efficient RISC process regenerates singlet excitons, which not only diminish optical absorption from the triplet state but also contribute to population inversion for lasing (Fig. 3h). Furthermore, the abundance of ZnO particles enhances the light scattering strength and extends the gain length, thereby facilitating random lasing emission (Fig. 5 a).

For collecting the lasing spectra, the CDs@ZnO-25 are excited by a laser beam at oblique angles. The random lasing signal is coupled into a fiber and then measured by the monochromator (Fig. 5b). As exhibited in Fig. 5c, a weak broad bump is formed around 510 nm when the pump power is low. After the excitation energy exceeds a certain value (threshold at 28.95 kW/cm2), sharp peaks (lasing modes) emerge from the spectra. Meanwhile, a knot in the input–output curve indicates that the stimulated emission starts to be dominate at this power (Fig. 5f). As the pump power is further increased, the intense lasing modes can be observed and randomly distribute over the spectra. As a result of random scattering, multi-directionality is a general feature of random lasing.[54] The direction perpendicular to the CDs@ZnO-25 is served as 0°, like Fig. 5b. We then investigate the lasing spectra collected at different angles ranging from 0° to 45° (Fig. S21). Different peaks positions are obtained from the spectra at different angles, proving the multi-directionality of the random lasing.

In the case of CDs@ZnO-5, the reduced content of ZnO leads to the diminished RISC process, consequently resulting in a lower optical gain for lasing emission. The CDs@ZnO-5 thereby present a higher threshold compared to CDs@ZnO-25 (Fig. 5d and g). Nevertheless, no matter how the pump power increases, no obvious lasing modes are witnessed from the spectra of CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 5e and h). Owing to the non-radiative transition, dissipation of excitons takes place simultaneously in both the singlet state and triplet state. As a result, the optical gain is compromised, falling short of the optical loss, therefore inadequate to support the random lasing emission.

Benefiting from the excellent thermochromic afterglow properties, CDs@ZnO-25 are anticipated to find applications in anti-counterfeiting and encryption. To augment the security level of anti-counterfeiting, CDs@ZnO-5 and CDs@SiO2 are also integrated into the anti-counterfeiting patterns. It is worth mentioning that, the CDs@ZnO-5 are employed for its nearly unchanged afterglow color across temperature variations (Fig. S11b), while the CDs@SiO2 serve as a complementary component, which can merely exhibit fluorescence emission, to bolster the security level of the anti-counterfeiting.

As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the anti-counterfeiting patterns are segmented into nine distinct sections, each numbered sequentially. Modules No. 1, 3, 5 are comprised of CDs@ZnO-25, modules No. 2, 4, 8 are constructed from CDs@ZnO-5, and modules No. 6, 7, 9 are fabricated by using CDs@SiO2. Hence, nine blue modules of the patterns can be observed with different brightness upon 405 nm excitation. As the temperature rises, the afterglow color of the patterns undergoes a series of transitions, as shown in Fig. 6b. After removing the light source, yellow afterglow is exhibited in modules No. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8 initially, while the light of modules No. 6, 7, 9 prepared by CDs@SiO2 disappears. With increasing the temperature, the afterglow of modules No. 1, 3, 5 gradually switches to green from yellow, concurrent with the diminution and final disappearance of the yellow afterglow from modules No. 2, 4, 8 (Fig. 6d). Interestingly, the blue fluorescence arises from the combination of the 510 nm emission from CDs@ZnO-25 and the 405 nm excitation light, thereby enriching the color diversity of the anti-counterfeiting patterns. The intricate color combinations thereby significantly bolster the security of the anti-counterfeiting patterns.

To augment the security levels of anti-counterfeiting, we introduce a strategy for implementing the lasing-based anti-counterfeiting technology. With the obvious difference that whether the lasing emission is generated, it is easy to define 1/0 for optical coding. As displayed in Fig. 6c, three types of materials are arranged as mentioned before. Here, we demonstrate two cases under different excitation energy: (Ⅰ) When the patterns are excited by high power laser (pump energy density exceeds both the lasing threshold of CDs@ZnO-25 and CDs@ZnO-5), the sections composed of these two materials exhibit lasing emission, which are defined as “1″. On the contrary, no lasing emission is observed from the sections built by CDs@SiO2, which are specified as “0”; It is clear that lasing modes are randomly distributed on the spectra of CDs@ZnO-25 and CDs@ZnO-5 collected under excitation of 278 kW/cm2 laser. And only spontaneous emission spectra can be obtained from CDs@SiO2 (Fig. S22a); (Ⅱ) As the patterns are excited by high power laser (pump power levels that fall between the lasing threshold of CDs@ZnO-25 and CDs@ZnO-5), lasing emission is exclusively witnessed from CDs@ZnO-25. Hence, the modules constituted by CDs@ZnO-25 are served as “1”, while the rest are set as “0”. The low pump energy is inadequate for CDs@ZnO-5 to generate random laser. Consequently, the CDs@ZnO-5 manifest the spontaneous emission spectrum analogous to that collected from CDs@SiO2 (Fig. S22b). To further elucidate the practical applicability of CDs@ZnO-25, we have developed an additional two anti-counterfeiting motifs. Fig. 6e illustrates the pattern of a yellow leaf, fabricated from CDs@ZnO-25, at an ambient temperature of 30 ℃. Upon increasing the temperature, the leaf embarks on a journey of reinvigoration, transitioning from its withered state to one of rejuvenated splendor, symbolizing a rebirth of vitality. Another one is the QR code, which is ubiquitous in our daily lives. By altering the operational temperatures, the color of the QR code can transition from yellow to green. To demonstrate the applicability of CDs@ZnO-25 in the domain of information encryption, we have constructed a cryptographic pattern utilizing CDs@ZnO-5 and CDs@SiO2 as interference. As depicted in Fig. 6f, under excitation, the pattern reveals an erroneous message 'Polite youth'. Upon termination of the excitation source, the absence of afterglow emission from CDs@SiO2 results in the pattern displaying 'Pol e you', which remains incorrect. It is only when the pattern is subjected to a temperature of 90 ℃ that the true information 'Pol y u' becomes discernible, attributable to the significantly diminished afterglow emission from CDs@ZnO-5 at elevated temperatures. Thus, by manipulating the stimulus conditions, the concealed information can be accurately decoded.

The Fig. S23 exhibits the similar configuration and intensity of afterglow spectra, indicating the good water resistance of CDs@ZnO-25. Both the fluorescence and phosphorescence can maintain their brightness even after the CDs@ZnO-25 immersed into the water (the insets of Fig. S23). Additionally, superior stability can be observed from Fig. S24, in which the afterglow emission intensity of CDs@ZnO-25 only shows slight fluctuation over an extended period. And the afterglow spectra keep almost unchanged during this time (the inset of Fig. S24).

Overall, we propose a strategic approach to multi-levels anti-counterfeiting that leverages thermochromic afterglow and random lasing. Following changing the operation temperature, the anti-counterfeiting patterns display different afterglow colors combinations. Based on the apparent difference between the PL spectra and lasing spectra, optical coding can be realized by altering the pump power. This approach is designed to significantly elevate the complexity and difficulty of replication, thereby providing a robust and advanced level of security for the authentication of products.

In summary, we introduce a novel versatile platform, in which the chemical encapsulation within the ZnO multi-core@shell structure enables both RTP and TADF emissions from CDs, for the first time. This platform exhibits universality for CDs with diverse structures, offering a promising avenue for developing multi-colored afterglow CD materials. The formation of the ZnO multi-core@shell structure occurs through the hydrolysis and dehydration process, imposing a three-dimensional spatial constraint on the CDs. Simultaneously, a large number of hydroxyl groups are produced, reacting with the carboxyl groups from the CDs to form robust C-O-Zn bonds. This combined effect of confinement within the multi-core@shell structure and immobilization of C-O-Zn bonds stabilizes the triplet state of the CDs, impeding nonradiative transitions. Furthermore, due to the narrow energy band gap (ΔEst) and efficient RISC process, both RTP and TADF are achieved within a single material. The RISC process also facilitates the regeneration of singlet excitons from the triplet state, aiding in the realization of population inversion necessary for lasing emission. By incorporating RTP, TADF, and random lasing, the CDs@ZnO exhibit capabilities suitable for applications requiring advanced security features such as anti-counterfeiting and encryption technologies.
